caveats: a: BLS data cover NAICS 325 and do not separate the two chemistry families or the outsourced-service subset.
a: ILO exposure is not an observed displacement rate and focuses on generative AI.
a: Existing distributed control and laboratory automation must be excluded from the remaining opportunity.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation in eligible NAICS 325194 firms.
rho: Implementation may differ materially between continuous cyclic-intermediate plants and smaller batch wood-chemical operations.
e: The provided firm count is a margin-bridged estimate and is very small, so classifying one firm differently has a large effect.
e: Public descriptions rarely distinguish proprietary product sales from true third-party toll processing.
s5: The Census owner-age evidence is cross-industry, based on 2018, and counts responding owners rather than firms.
s5: Aging ownership does not establish sale intent, transferability, or a completed control transaction.
q: No public contract dataset establishes benefit sharing for eligible firms.
q: Capture may differ sharply between bespoke modified rosin products and benchmarked cyclic intermediates.
d5: Petrochemical feedstocks are a broader and different product population than this combined NAICS code.
d5: Gum and wood chemical demand can diverge from cyclic intermediates, making one industry ratio structurally imprecise.
d5: Constant-quality outsourced-service demand is not directly observed.
o: The estimate does not protect specific chemicals from substitution or regulation, which is reflected in d5.
o: Operator-required work can migrate to a larger integrated supplier even when the physical step persists.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Cyclic Crude, Intermediate, and Gum and Wood Chemical Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial customers purchase recurring batches, campaigns, or volumes under product specifications, with revenue based on conversion fees, unit prices, or formulas that can pass through feedstock and energy costs.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines petroleum- or coal-derived cyclic intermediates with gum and wood chemicals and also mixes commodity and specialty operations. The lens retains only recurring outsourced toll or custom processing across those chemistries so the business model is coherent, while preserving the code's heterogeneous end-market risk.
lens.excluded_activities: Integrated refinery, steel, pulp, or forestry operations; plants selling only proprietary commodity output; captive facilities; shell entities; non-control financing vehicles; and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market producers that repeatedly provide toll, custom, or specification-driven distillation, fractionation, synthesis, recovery, or modification of cyclic intermediates and gum or wood chemicals for external industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all chemical manufacturing rather than NAICS 325194 contract processors, and ILO reports global GenAI task exposure by broad occupation. I assign greater exposure to commercial, scheduling, laboratory-documentation, quality, regulatory, purchasing, and engineering-analysis work and lower exposure to physical plant operation, maintenance, loading, and sampling. The broad chemical occupation mix may overstate exposure for continuous cyclic-crude plants but understate document work at small specialty gum and wood processors.
caveats: BLS data cover NAICS 325 and do not separate the two chemistry families or the outsourced-service subset.
ILO exposure is not an observed displacement rate and focuses on generative AI.
Existing distributed control and laboratory automation must be excluded from the remaining opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from eligible cyclic-intermediate and gum or wood contract processors, identifying already automated tasks and actual time by workflow, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The eligible model combines physical conversion with meaningful specification, laboratory, customer, and regulatory work. AI can assist document review, batch-history analysis, planning, quoting, and knowledge retrieval, but hazardous physical operations and accountable product release remain human-intensive.

d5.bridge: EIA's product-supplied series measures broad U.S. petrochemical feedstocks, not NAICS 325194 outsourced service quantity, and it excludes much of the gum and wood segment. Annual product supplied fell from 115.776 million barrels in 2019 to 74.983 million in 2024, while EIA's 2026 outlook expects long-run bulk-chemical feedstock use to help drive industrial petroleum growth. I translate these conflicting broad signals into slightly lower base five-year quantity with a wide cyclical range. Petrochemical data may overstate weakness if eligible firms serve differentiated wood-derived niches and may overstate growth if new capacity accrues to integrated producers.
caveats: Petrochemical feedstocks are a broader and different product population than this combined NAICS code.
Gum and wood chemical demand can diverge from cyclic intermediates, making one industry ratio structurally imprecise.
Constant-quality outsourced-service demand is not directly observed.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level shipment volumes, import penetration, customer end-market forecasts, contract backlogs, capacity changes, and recurring-volume histories for eligible plants would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Cyclic intermediates and wood-derived chemicals remain inputs into physical downstream products, but demand is exposed to industrial cycles, imports, refinery and steel economics, substitution, paper-market change, and environmental restrictions. The recent broad feedstock series is weak, while longer-run energy projections support continued physical chemical use.

caveats: The provided firm count is a margin-bridged estimate and is very small, so classifying one firm differently has a large effect.
Public descriptions rarely distinguish proprietary product sales from true third-party toll processing.
e.change_evidence: A classification of all 12 dataset firms using customer contracts, ownership of feedstock and finished product, toll or conversion-fee revenue, integration with upstream facilities, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Census defines the code to include wood or gum distillation, coal-tar distillation, wood and gum chemicals, and cyclic intermediates from petroleum or natural gas. Many establishments are integrated product manufacturers, but niche custom fractionation, recovery, and specification-driven processing can fit the recurring outsourced-service lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325194 ($291,691,690) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325194 ($5,683,585,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $970,558 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325194); class <5: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,902,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $702,850 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,826,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $869,253 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,580,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,725,493 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $25,888,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,662,519 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $167,780,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,217,178 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $286,513,647 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,601,108 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 12

caveats: The estimate does not protect specific chemicals from substitution or regulation, which is reflected in d5.
Operator-required work can migrate to a larger integrated supplier even when the physical step persists.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy studies, announced captive capacity, supplier-consolidation records, formulation-substitution plans, and autonomous-plant evidence showing removal of external accountability would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical distillation, reaction, fractionation, modification, testing, safe handling, and accountable release. AI and software may reduce coordination and analysis labor but cannot supply the chemical; displacement is more likely through customer insourcing, integrated-plant consolidation, or material substitution.

caveats: No public contract dataset establishes benefit sharing for eligible firms.
Capture may differ sharply between bespoke modified rosin products and benchmarked cyclic intermediates.
q.change_evidence: A contract sample showing term, renewal cadence, feedstock and energy indices, open-book provisions, yield guarantees, gainsharing, minimum volumes, and historical repricing after process improvements would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Customer qualification, niche process knowledge, hazardous handling, and specification consistency can protect savings, especially when AI improves yield, quality, or turnaround. Cost-index formulas, open-book tolling, repeat bids, substitute materials, and concentrated industrial procurement can transfer visible productivity gains to customers.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in eligible NAICS 325194 firms.
Implementation may differ materially between continuous cyclic-intermediate plants and smaller batch wood-chemical operations.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-site pilots measuring accepted AI outputs, exception and hallucination rates, labor hours, validation cost, and audit findings in scheduling, quality, maintenance, and regulatory workflows would materially change implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Office, planning, quality, and knowledge workflows can be implemented within five years, while process-safety validation, customer qualification, environmental reporting, cybersecurity, fragmented plant data, and rare failure events constrain operational deployment. EPA includes cyclic crude and intermediate production in its chemical-manufacturing area-source standards.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner age for responding U.S. employer businesses across industries, not completed control transfers among eligible 325194 processors. I use the 51% age-55-or-older share only as succession pressure, then reduce it for the five-year window, integrated and institutional ownership, internal succession, non-sale exits, buyer diligence, permits, and environmental liabilities. The cross-industry proxy likely overstates owner-driven transactions for capital-intensive chemical plants.
caveats: The Census owner-age evidence is cross-industry, based on 2018, and counts responding owners rather than firms.
Aging ownership does not establish sale intent, transferability, or a completed control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: Closed chemical-sector transaction records, state and EPA permit ownership changes, and interviews with specialty-chemical advisers and owners of eligible plants over the prior five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Independent niche processors may face owner succession, but qualifying transfers depend on a transferable customer book, permits, feedstock access, environmental condition, and process know-how. The tiny eligible population and code heterogeneity make transaction timing especially uncertain.

principal_driver: Recurring customer specifications and difficult physical processing can create sticky outsourced relationships and useful AI-enabled quality and planning workflows.
principal_weakness: The tiny, heterogeneous eligible population and weak direct demand evidence make firm availability and five-year quantity unusually uncertain.
